flows: datadisplay -> localuser — type=association; label=""; explanation="";
datadisplay -> realtimedatabase — type=association; label=""; explanation="";
localuser -> authentication — type=association; label=""; explanation="";
localuser -> firebase — type=association; label=""; explanation="";
flow 1: datadisplay -> localuser -> authentication -> firebase
the diagram represents a system with a data display component that interacts with a local user, a real-time database, and a firebase service.
the data display component is responsible for displaying data to the local user.
the local user interacts with the authentication component to authenticate before accessing the firebase service.
the real-time database is used to store and retrieve data in real-time.
the firebase service is the backend service that provides the data storage and retrieval functionality.
the system is designed to handle data in real-time, as indicated by the real-time database component.
the authentication component ensures that only authorized users can access the firebase service.
guidelines by diagram type (keep it generic):
for class diagrams:
* treat each class box as a component with kind="class".
* in the component bullet, list all attributes and operations exactly as written, including type and return type if visible.
* in relationships / links, list associations, aggregations, compositions, and inheritances between classes (use "association", "inheritance", etc.).
* main flows can describe likely collaboration between classes based only on visible methods and relationships (use "association", "inheritance", etc.).
for activity diagrams:
* components are actions, decisions, merges, initial nodes, final nodes, etc.
* relationships / links correspond to control-flow arrows.
* main flows should be explicit sequences like: "avvio app" -> "utente registrato con successo al servizio".
for sequence diagrams:
* main flows should be explicit sequences like: "avvvio app" -> "utente registrato con successo al servizio".

user -> admin — type=association; label="login"; explanation="user logs in to admin system"
user -> product — type=association; label="addtocart"; explanation="user adds a product to their cart"
user -> cart — type=association; label="addproduct"; explanation="user adds a product to their cart"
user -> checkout — type=association; label="checkout"; explanation="user checks out from the system"
admin -> product — type=association; label="addtocart"; explanation="admin adds a product to their cart"
admin -> checkout — type=association; label="checkout"; explanation="admin checks out from the system"
flow 1: user -> admin -> checkout
flow 2: admin -> user -> product -> cart -> checkout
the system has two types of users: a user and an admin.
users can log in and out, add and remove products from their cart, and check out.
admins can log in and out, add and remove products from their cart, and check out.
the system has a product that can be added to and removed from a cart.
the system has a cart that can be populated with products.
the system has a checkout that processes the user's cart.
